metadata.title:
COmPERS' REVIEW OF


THE LABOR OUTLOOK

pages.raw_text:
all the II months. May was the
busiest month, our tables showing
that less than l per cent of the
union labor was that month with
out employment. During May Of
1905 the percentage was between l
and 2.


In midsummer. in August. 1905,
the percentage of unemployed rose
to per cent; in 1906 the proper
tion kept below 2 per cent until
September, when it rose a little
above per cent. In the fall again
the last of October, in both years,
the proportion of unemployed
dropped to less than 1 per cent. In
December, 1906, there was letup
again to per cent; but the new
year saw. as We have seen. all but
p per cent at work. and this per
centage has been lessening up to
date.


These percentages tell double
story. They reflect the conditions
of the labor market as to strike dis
turbances, and as to industrial dis
turbances. Any widespread strike
throws number of people out of
employment and finds expression in
these figures. So. any letting up of
good times. any slowing down of
the wheels of industry, increases
the number of the unemployed. and
affects these percentages.


The percentages are the result
of reports made to me as the edi-
tor of the American Federationist.
They are based on returns from
some $50 local unions The trades
are diversified. and are sufficiently
typical so that the figures unques-
tonably represent the general con-
ditions as they prevail.
